requirement.original: Make hooks/lib/bash_context_strip.py (and by extension hooks/pretool-bash-safety.sh) context-aware: only flag dangerous tokens when they appear in a position that is actually executable shell context (sh -c / bash -c payload, shell heredoc body), NOT when they appear only as string literals inside Python heredocs, codex prompt bodies, argument values, comments, or non-shell heredocs.
requirement.clarified: In _process_segment(), when the command word is a non-shell script interpreter (python3, node, perl, ruby, php), strip unquoted word-kind argument tokens (kind=='word') after cmd_idx that do not contain shell metacharacters (<>()backtick), eliminating false positive rm/killall blocks from argv like 'python3 cleanup.py rm /tmp/old'.
requirement.what: Add _SCRIPT_INTERPS branch in _process_segment() of hooks/lib/bash_context_strip.py to strip word tokens after script interpreter command words
requirement.why: Unquoted argv to non-shell interpreters passes through verbatim causing false positive danger-token matches; e.g. 'python3 manage.py rm session-123' triggers the rm-block hook erroneously

included: hooks/lib/bash_context_strip.py: _process_segment() word-token stripping for script interps
hooks/lib/bash_context_strip.py: optional _is_script_interp() helper for versioned names

success_criteria: python3 cleanup.py rm /tmp/old does not trigger rm-block false positive
node script.js pkill happy does not trigger killall-block false positive
python3 script.py <(rm /tmp/x) still exposes rm (process substitution not masked)
bash -c 'rm /tmp/foo' still exposes rm (shell interp path unchanged)
python3 script.py && rm /tmp/x still exposes rm in second segment
constraints: Word tokens containing shell metacharacters <, >, (, ), backtick must NOT be stripped (process substitution safety)
Fix is bounded to _process_segment() + set definition; no other functions modified
Change must not widen scope to pretool-bash-safety.sh

root_cause_analysis.symptom: Commands like 'python3 manage.py rm session-123' or 'node server.js killall happy' trigger the safety hook's danger-token patterns erroneously because rm/killall appear as plain text in the stripped output
root_cause_analysis.root_cause: In _process_segment() at hooks/lib/bash_context_strip.py:311, the token loop's else-branch appends word-kind tokens verbatim: 'out.append(text)'. For non-shell interpreter commands, this keeps all positional argv (script name, subcommands, path arguments) visible in the stripped text, allowing danger-token regexes to match.
root_cause_analysis.root_cause_commit: N/A — architectural gap in initial implementation, not a regression
root_cause_analysis.why_introduced: The generic _process_segment() branch was written to handle arbitrary command arg stripping: quoted args (single/double) are stripped, subst tokens preserved, word tokens kept (safe assumption for most commands). No special case was written for non-shell interpreter commands whose argv is pure data.
root_cause_analysis.why_problematic: Unquoted subcommands and path arguments to python3/node/perl/ruby commands can coincidentally match danger-token patterns: 'rm' as a Django management command, 'killall' as a script subcommand, 'kill' as an argument name.
root_cause_analysis.timeline: Present since initial implementation of bash_context_strip.py; not introduced by any recent commit
observations: Read hooks/lib/bash_context_strip.py:299-312 — confirmed else-branch 'out.append(text)' for kind=='word' (inferred from source)
Confirmed _SHELL_INTERPS = {'bash', 'sh', 'zsh', 'dash'} at line 21 — no _SCRIPT_INTERPS exists (inferred from source)
Confirmed tokenizer stop chars at line 195 do not include '(' — '<(rm' becomes single word token (inferred from source)
Verified by running strip_non_executable_contexts test via file-based script that python3 cleanup.py rm /tmp/old keeps rm visible (observed)
root_cause_analysis.localization_evidence: Direct read of _process_segment() token loop at lines 299-312; confirmed the word-kind else-branch at line 311. Test run confirmed false positive: strip_non_executable_contexts('python3 cleanup.py rm /tmp/old') returns text containing 'rm' (visible to danger-token regex).

requirements_decomposition.source_phrase: only flag dangerous tokens when they appear in a position that is actually executable shell context
requirements_decomposition.acceptance_criterion: strip_non_executable_contexts('python3 cleanup.py rm /tmp/old') output does not match (^|[ \t;|&(])rm\s

requirements_decomposition.source_phrase: NOT when they appear only as string literals inside Python heredocs, codex prompt bodies, argument values
requirements_decomposition.acceptance_criterion: Unquoted argv tokens (word kind) to python3/node/perl/ruby are stripped like single-quoted args when they do not contain shell metacharacters
All 40 tests in hooks/tests/test_bash_safety_context.py pass after the change

component_chain.evidence: Read pretool-bash-safety.sh lines 764-777 confirmed python3 invocation of bash_context_strip.py and COMMAND_CONTEXT_STRIPPED assignment

component_chain.role: strip_non_executable_contexts() entry point; calls _split_top_level(), _process_heredocs(), _process_segment() per segment
component_chain.evidence: Read full file confirmed function structure and token loop at lines 299-312

pre_existing_guards.purpose: Identifies shell interpreters whose -c payload must be recursively analyzed as executable (handled by _process_shell_interp before _process_segment)
Identifies commands whose args must be preserved (not stripped) so danger patterns can still match their targets

setup.reason: non-UI -- hook; cycle does not produce (1) rendered UI changes, (2) browser interaction, (3) Playwright invocation, (4) screenshot evidence, or (5) any change to user-triggered code paths in the UI pipeline sense. The file modified is a PreToolUse safety hook implementation (hooks/lib/bash_context_strip.py).

evidence.observed: Commands like 'python3 manage.py rm session-123' or 'node server.js killall happy' trigger the hook's danger-token block erroneously as false positives

measured.value: strip_non_executable_contexts('python3 cleanup.py rm /tmp/old') returns text where 'rm' remains as a standalone word token matching danger regex

expected.value: Unquoted argv tokens after script interpreter command word should be stripped to empty string, same as single-quoted args
expected.source: By analogy with existing kind=='single' branch at line 302: out.append('')

evidence.gap: One code path: else-branch at line 311 appends word tokens verbatim for all command types including script interpreters; needs guard for _SCRIPT_INTERPS with metacharacter exception

reference_source.description: Direct read of hooks/lib/bash_context_strip.py in this session; existing code pattern at line 302 (kind=='single': out.append('')) is the authoritative template for the fix

novelty_check.rationale: No prior attempts; this is the first cycle addressing this specific false positive class

_risk_level_doc: SMALL tier, single file modification, logic change bounded to one else-branch in _process_segment(); no infrastructure, auth, schema, or public API impact.

codex_consult.feedback_summary: Codex produced 5 findings. Finding 1 (process substitution masking) is a valid blocker: naive word-token stripping would hide '<(rm ...' because '(' is not a tokenizer stop char, making '<(rm' a single word token. Finding 4 (versioned interpreter names) is in-scope minor. Findings 2, 3, 5 are out-of-scope or nitpick.

findings.issue: Naive strip of all word tokens after script-interp cmd word would hide rm inside process substitution '<(rm /tmp/x)' because '<(rm' tokenizes as a single word token
findings.proposed_fix: Guard: only strip word tokens that do NOT contain shell metacharacters (<>()backtick). Tokens containing those chars are preserved.
findings.rationale: Verified correct by inspecting tokenizer stop chars at line 195 which exclude '('. The hook pattern (^|[ \t;|&(])rm\s uses '(' as a left-boundary anchor, confirming '<(rm' would trigger the pattern if visible.

findings.issue: Backslash-escaped control operators (e.g. echo foo\; rm /tmp/x) bypass _split_top_level()
findings.rationale: Pre-existing limitation outside the stated user requirement. Codex marked this as out-of-scope for the current fix.

findings.issue: Wrapper chains like 'env python3 script.py rm x' — _find_command_word may not unwrap to python3
findings.rationale: _find_command_word() already unwraps _WRAPPER_PREFIXES = {env, time, sudo, nice, ionice, nohup, timeout, doas, run0} per reading of the source. The wrapper chain case is already handled.

findings.issue: Versioned interpreter names like python3.12, node20 not matched by plain set membership
findings.rationale: Valid edge case; helper is low-cost and adds meaningful coverage for real-world interpreter invocations.

findings.issue: Example in draft used 'killall-proc happy-svc' (hyphenated) which is not a real hook trigger; real false positive is 'python3 cleanup.py rm /tmp/old'
findings.proposed_fix: Use real confirmed false positive examples in AC
findings.rationale: Corrected in AC: AC1 uses 'python3 cleanup.py rm /tmp/old', AC3 uses 'node script.js pkill happy' (unhyphenated, matching hook pattern).

codex_consult.feedback_incorporated: Finding 1 (blocker) applied: word token stripping now conditioned on absence of shell metacharacters <>()`backtick. Finding 4 applied: _is_script_interp() helper recommended. Finding 5 applied: AC examples updated to confirmed false positives. Findings 2 and 3 logged as out-of-scope/already-handled.
